text.title: Churn Cohort Analysis Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every Churn Cohort Analysis application must enforce. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: Split churn cohorts by activation status (activated within 7 days, activated 8-30 days, never activated). Each segment has a different churn curve.
rule.rationale: Aggregate churn hides 2-4 distinct curves; segmentation reveals the actual problem.

rule.statement: Identify ≥2 behavioral leading indicators (e.g., login frequency drop, feature stop-use, support ticket spike) that predict churn ≥14 days ahead.
rule.rationale: Cancellations are lagging signals; intervene only on leading.

rule.statement: Every save-flow tested has a hold-out control group (≥10% of at-risk cohort). Without control, you cannot tell if the save-flow worked or the user would have stayed anyway.
rule.rationale: Selection bias in save-flows: users who answer save-emails are pre-disposed to stay; control isolates effect.

rule.statement: Use a 90-day lookback window for cohort comparison. Shorter windows (30 days) over-fit recent churn waves; longer (180+) blurs the signal.
rule.rationale: Industry default; 90 days is the sweet spot for SaaS churn analysis.

rule.statement: Document the active cohort segments + their churn rates in a quarterly memo. Trends only emerge with 3-4 quarters of stable definitions.
rule.rationale: Segment definitions drift silently; quarterly snapshots create comparability over time.

rule.statement: If any Skip-If clause from AGENTS.md fires OR any precondition fails, STOP applying this methodology and route to the correct one. Producing an artefact when the methodology does not apply is forbidden.
rule.rationale: Mis-application is the most common methodology-routing failure observed in audits. An explicit STOP rule prevents the agent from synthesizing outputs for the wrong instrument.
